4. Section 4: Members of our consortium  
4.1. Participants (applicants)  
Partner CiaoTech s.r.l / PNO Group B.V.  
Short Name PNO  
Type Large Enterprise  
Role Coordinator and Innovation Management services  
Country Italy  
Website www.pnoconsultants.com  
Logo  
Description of our legal entity  
CIAOTECH s.r.l., a company 100% owned by the PNO Group B.V. (hereafter PNO), is the largest independent public funding and innovation consultancy in Europe, with 30 years of hands-on expertise with more than 600 innovation projects funded per year in most EU countries, annually raising approximately 1 Billion Euro for our clients. Created in 1985, PNO is a high-growth knowledge-intensive company, supporting over 2,000 clients throughout Europe, annually developing over 250 European consortia. PNO has offices in 7 European countries and in Israel, employing around 500 professionals including scientists, engineers, consultants, a Brussels policy advisory service, as well as financial and legal experts, with consolidated experience in innovation processes and funding in international working environments. The company specializes in Grants and Innovation Management, providing support services in Innovation processes, Technology Transfer, IT solutions and funding for research, development and innovation. PNO “Innovation Management” services deliver high-quality support to large companies, SMEs, Universities, Research Institutes, Associations and clusters in the full cycle of the innovation process, including:  
• Analysing, defining and planning innovation processes;  
• Building innovation networks, partnerships and projects;  
• Managing Projects and driving innovation.  
PNO projects involve both small, medium-sized and multinational industries, as well as Universities and Research institutes, amongst which are European and international leaders such as Amsterdam Schiphol Airport, BASF, Booking.com, DOW Chemical, CERN, Finmeccanica/Selex, Fraunhofer Institute, Harbours of Rotterdam and Antwerp, HP, Intel, Microsoft, Philips, Procter & Gamble, Solvay, TomTom, the Universities of Bordeaux, Ghent, Leuven, Rome, Naples, Utrecht, Warwick, Politecnico of Milano, Turin and many more. PNO is a key partner and advisor to the European Technology Platform (ETP) SusChem and the European Council for the Chemical Industry (CEFIC) and PNO recently (2019) co-developed the latest Strategic Innovation and Research Agenda. PNO has furthermore supported A.SPIRE in the development of its first Roadmap and recently (2019) the annual monitoring report on SPIRE funded projects. Furthermore, PNO manages several European EC funded network projects to build communities of valuable SMEs, large industries, Universities and Research Institutes, to further innovation in specific sectors, such as the bio-based product market (BIOCHEM), transport (SMART), security (OSMOSIS), open source and embedded software (SHARE), Chemical Regions for Resource Efficiency (R4R) to name a few. Leveraging on our insights and knowledge on innovation methodologies and practices, PNO increasingly supports our clients as partners in strategic European research, development and innovation projects, to carry out market and functional analyses, develop and implement road mapping, dissemination and exploitation strategies and business plans and support professional project management. PNO participates in a significant number of European H2020 projects that relate to the topic of the AI-CUBE one. Specifically, PNO has recently concluded the INSPIRE Coordination and Support Action (CSA) targeted at analysing the future of the European process industry, more particularly how novel technologies, such as AI, may lead to novel Business Models for the eight SPIRE process industries. The Business Model Innovation Game developed together with SPIRE partner TNO, as well as the research needs paper from INSPIRE, will provide the basis for a dedicated AI-CUBE Business Model Game which can help to focus the AI and BD roadmap developments. Furthermore, PNO participated as a partner in the NEXT-NET Coordination and Support Action, targeted at analysing long-term megatrend scenarios for the process – manufacturing – logistics supply chain, and developing a research roadmap for the supply chain of the future. The output of INSPIRE and NEXT NET will provide direct knowledge and tool base for our AI-CUBE project.  
Main tasks and responsibilities within our project  
PNO will be the coordinator of our project and therefore lead WP6. Also, PNO will lead WP5 “Dissemination, Communication and Exploitation”, that is designed to maximize awareness around our project results and create a sustainable dissemination and exploitation platform, based on stakeholder involvement at regional, national and European levels. Also, PNO will technically contribute to the other work packages with our expertise.  
Profiles of persons primarily responsible for carrying out the proposed activities  
Ronald Weerdmeester  
Profile https://www.linkedin.com/in/ronweerdmeester/  
Several decades of consulting experience in Innovation strategy, visions, roadmaps and strategic research and innovation agendas development. Ron WEERDMEESTER currently works as PNO Group Innovation Manager. Graduated in law at Rijksuniversiteit Groningen and holds a Certificate Degree in "Information Systems in Organisations", from Boston University. Member of the board of PNO’s units PNO Innovation and CiaoTech. Expert in innovation strategy, intelligence and innovation funding for industries, academia and public organisations. 25 years’ experience in the development of technology development projects with a European dimension and EU funding (from FP4 to HORIZON 2020). Development of more than 100 successful EU projects. Co-author of multiple visions, roadmaps and strategic research and innovation agendas for sector organisations (process industry, chemistry, bioeconomy, water). Project manager of various Economic and Technological Intelligence actions, assisting industry in their innovation needs, developing European networks. Regular training to SMEs, multinationals and research organisations from various European countries, on issues such as European research and innovation policies, open-innovation project building, project management in networks, technology transfer and the development of European partnerships.  
Chiara Eleonora De Marco  
Profile www.linkedin.com/in/chiaraedemarco  
Chiara (female) is an Innovation Consultant in the Strategic Innovation Services Team of CiaoTech/ PNO Group. She holds an International PhD in Innovation Management from the Scuola Superiore Sant’Anna, Pisa (IT), a Master’s Degree in International Relations and International Protection of Human Rights, and another one in Diplomatic Studies, both awarded from the Società Italiana per l’Organizzazione Internazionale (SIOI) of Rome (IT). She graduated summa cum laude in International Law from LUMSA University, Rome (IT). Chiara has working experiences both in National and International environments, embracing academia, public and private sector. She was Postdoctoral Scholar at Haas School of Business of the University of California, Berkeley (US), working on Corporate Capability Building Programs for Digital Transformation, and at the Institute of Management of Scuola Superiore Sant’Anna (Pisa, IT), conducting research and leading national and international projects on open innovation, digital business model and strategy, innovation policy, small business R&D, research spinoffs, technology and knowledge transfer. Over her years in Academia, she conducted research on and led consultancy projects for the private sector (both SMEs and large corporations) and the public one (European Commission, JRC), joining theory and practice of open innovation to identify and help address bottlenecks and pain points in corporate strategy, but also recommendations for decision makers in the field of innovation. As a member of the Organizing Committee of the World Open Innovation Conference (https://woic.corporateinnovation.berkeley.edu/), she is in charge of the management of Industry Sessions and Workshops. Chiara disseminated her research work in several international conferences and forums, joining the international innovation community across the EU and the US. Her work has been published in peer-reviewed, international academic journals, EU publications, national publications, specialized blogs and newspapers.  
List of up to 5 relevant publications, and/or products, services, or other achievements relevant to the call content  
Analysing, defining and planning innovation processes  
• Market, stakeholder & Technology Intelligence. Provides a helicopter view on who’s doing what in the EU and beyond, benchmarking target technology with other technologies and solutions, analysing and identifying the essential markets that need to be targeted;  
• Systematic Innovation using advanced methodologies (eg: TRIZ). Discovers new technologies, partners and markets, structuring and driving open innovation processes in multi-stakeholder contexts, or for individual clients;  
• Vision and roadmap development. Shows where to focus, organising, supporting and reporting on vision and innovation roadmaps of interest.  
Building innovation networks, partnerships and projects  
• Develop innovation projects, proposals and consortia. Builds innovation projects with international partners supported by public funding, bringing the best funding expertise, relevant networks and hands-on support;  
• Effective linking to innovation ideas, partners and funding across sectors and Europe. Enables becoming a partner in projects supported by public funding to discover or bring innovation and technology to new markets, unlocking networks of projects and stakeholders.  
Relevant Publications  
• The Value of Water, Multiple Waters, for multiple purposes and users, Ron Weerdmeester et al. (2016) – WssTP, The European Water Platform, URL: http://wsstp.eu/publications/  
• Spire Roadmap, Ron Weerdmeester, Pablo Tello (2016) – SPIRE Sustainable Process Industry through Resource and Energy Efficiency, URL: www.spire2030.eu/what/walking-the-spire-roadmap/spire-Roadmap  
• Knowledge Management Maturity Model / Roadmap, Ron Weerdmeester, Chiara Pocaterra, Mark Hefke (2003) – Information Societies

Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. 4. We will convene a stakeholder community to provide insights on stakeholder perspectives and to gather regulatory input to gain an understanding of how to make the adoption and regulatory approvals of our AI-enhanced automated advanced therapy manufacturing system as efficient as possible; 5. We will develop and implement a plan to sustain our project assets by validating business models that take into consideration the health economics of engineered T-cell therapy. This translates into the following innovation, impact and exploitation objectives: Innovation Objectives  We will create an automated and robot-assisted cyberphysical manufacturing pilot to pave the way towards autonomous self-adaptive CAR-T manufacturing flow operating with hospital infrastructure.  We will compile a comprehensive database for patient-derived variability of CAR-T performance, from existing data of our clinical partners and high throughput experimentation (e.g. capturing impact of age, sex, genetic and disease background).  We will intensify data generation through the employment of robotics for analytical assays and sampling.  We will establish an AI-based digital twin for each batch capable of catching donor-derived variability.  We will implement AI-based control of bioreactor operation using multiple targets, i.e., optimizing bioreactor operation in terms of quantity of CAR-Ts and quality as defined through our CQA panel.  We will implement AI-powered smart scheduling and logistics for resources and adjacent processes.  We will create a holistic IoT infrastructure for AI-enabled manufacturing and logistic processes as well as data storage and tracking, which integrates into the hospital environment in a smart and seamless manner.  We will demonstrate the quality and efficacy of CAR-T products manufactured in our AIDPATH pilot. Impact Objectives  We will demonstrate that AI-based technologies are effective for resource planning and for automating the process of manufacturing advanced medical therapies by fully implementing an AIDPATH system in a hospital and delivering CAR-T cell therapies to at least ten patients during our project, thereby providing the scientific, clinical outcome, and cost-effectiveness rationale for supporting wider adoption of the ‘smart manufacturing’ hospital concept.  We will build the basis for a deployable platform in alignment with regulatory requirements and a cybersecurity by design approach so that pilots can be initiated in multiple hospitals towards the end of and after our project’s funding period.  We will transform the stakeholder community developed during our project into a European-led innovation ecosystem that attracts funding to build upon the outputs of our project, thereby driving the concept of the ‘smart manufacturing’ hospital forward. Exploitation and Dissemination Objectives  We will develop standards, building blocks, and guidelines for AI in the hospital that allow widespread adoption of AI and AIDPATH technology.  We will leverage our network of the innovation ecosystem consisting of companies, clinics, associations, regulatory bodies, and healthcare policymakers to communicate and educate about the AIDPATH results, gaining visibility, trust, and acceptance. 1.2 Relation to our work programme AIDPATH aligns excellently with the work programme topic “H2020 -DT-2018-2020: AI for the smart hospital of the future”: Call topic themes AIDPATH approach Health systems need to undergo adaptation if they are to adequately respond to future population health needs. (…). In particular, artificial intelligence and robotics have the potential to transform health and care facilities. Our focus on AI and robotics to support hospital-based manufacturing of advanced medical therapies addresses this challenge directly. One of the biggest future population needs is the ability to deploy advanced medical therapies for which society has invested heavily on a wide scale. The process of deploying many advanced medical therapies such as CAR-T cell therapy is complex. The complexity is further complicated by the need for the use of GMP processes. These factors taken together are exactly the types of processes that AI and robotics make better, more efficient, and more cost-effective. Piloting at scale is needed to prove the transformative impact of AI. Pilots need to be embedded in operational health and care settings. By the end of our project, there will be a fully functional AI automated CAR-T manufacturing fully operational in one of the leading CAR-T cell therapy clinical centres in Europe. Furthermore, our efforts in stakeholder engagement will be focused on identifying and readying other hospitals to begin their own pilots based upon the processes, software, and know-how that we develop. Pilots should enable or support clinical diagnosis and treatment, etc., carried out with clinical outcomes comparable to human-delivered procedures and with comparable results. The process of deploying many advanced medical therapies such as CAR-T cell therapy is complex and is currently a predominantly human-delivered procedure. The complexity is further complicated by the need for the use of GMP processes. CAR-T therapy, in particular, is particularly relevant because delivering the therapy to a patient requires clinical, diagnostic, care organization, and logistical aspects. We intend to deliver a platform that will make use of AI to make the process of delivering CAR-T cell therapy efficient and scalable. The technology will be tested and validated at the clinical site by the production of CAR-T cells, and results will be compared with the state-of-the-art process. AIDPATH 7 AI-based systems (....) have shown considerable promise so far; however, their effective use (…) depends on their successful integration (and acceptance). We place particular emphasis on building a participatory stakeholder community that will then be transformed into an innovation ecosystem in recognition of the fact that multiple different types of stakeholders have to accept and be engaged in attaining the future vision of personalized therapies being manufactured and administered at a SMART hospital. In addition, our AIs will be trustworthy by design, incorporating aspects such as transparency and accountability in accordance with the EU guidelines. We will address any aspect of health facility operations (e.g., diagnostics, treatments, logistical aspects). AIDPATH will deliver a holistic approach to smart manufacture in the hospital. Therefore, we will develop multiple AIs addressing four important areas: CAR-T cell production (1) process prediction, (2) process control, (3) manufacturing scheduling, and (4) resources planning. Solution performance will be measured against healthcare metrics. After deploying our AIDPATH IoT platform at UKW, we will test and validate it by performing trial runs on the actual platform. Bioprocess results such as yield, duration, and doublings will be compared to the performance of state-of-the-art processes. The generated product will be tested in vitro and in vivo for growth potential, phenotype, quality, functionality, transfection rates, efficacy, and biodistribution for benchmarking against manual processes. Development in cooperation with health and care facility partners Our consortium includes (1) world-class leaders in CAR-T therapies, including two clinical sites (UKW, FCRB) providing their insights from the hospital infrastructure standpoint and (2) a company with extensive background in hospital and healthcare data management (ORT). We will also set up an advisory board and build a stakeholder community that includes other hospital sites, healthcare policymakers, and related associations. Consideration of wider dimensions (e.g., impact on resources, staff) Our overall target will be to minimize the need for specialized operators during CAR-T production. Potential expert profiles will be needed to also interact and interface with medical doctors (regarding decision-making) and nurses (operational tasks). Efficient resource management will allow high resource availability and low waiting times. AI-based systems that combine digital and physical services Our project will implement an AI-integrated cyberphysical infrastructure that connects all devices of the CAR-T cell production plant to our AIDPATH software and data solutions in the sense of an internet of things. Services provided by the system include physical production and testing of the product, intelligent manufacturing control and scheduling, up to data storage and resource optimization. Individualized and integrated care solutions AIDPATH enables the production of truly personalized therapies completely in proximity to the patient integrated into the smart hospital of the future, including connection to hospital IT and logistics. Our AIs will allow tailoring of the process towards individual patient history and requirements. Integration of autonomous AI-driven smart components Our process control is smart and autonomous in a sense that it operates without human intervention necessary and governs all operations in the system (e.g., bioreactor parameter adaptation, resource rescheduling) through direct integration of our AI decisions. Direct and positive impact on effectiveness and efficiency in the healthcare context AIDPATH will allow provision of CAR-T cell products in better quality, tailored to the patient and produced in shorter time. The installation of the smart infrastructure at the hospital site reduces transportation times and enables lean production and resource planning for just-in-time logistics. The collection of extensive data sets on processes and products allows further optimization and extraction of insights on CAR-T therapy in the future. AIDPATH 8 Integration of health and care partners in the design of the pilot, the development of performance indicators, and access to the relevant operational environment A demonstrator pilot will be implemented at the clinical site at partner UKW with additional input from partner FCRB to ensure a technology that is deployable across sites. Partner ORTEC is currently engaged in supporting hospital resource planning with AI. Development of the technologies will integrate feedback from our advisory board and stakeholder-centric design principles. Demonstrate likely “at scale” benefits in efficiency or cost reduction Cost reduction is crucial for this type of product due to its current pricing (>200K/treatment). Our solution will directly impact this area since automation through robotics and optimization through the use of AI will result in products that will be produced at lower cost. The possibility to parallelize production and intelligently schedule processes will ensure intelligent utilization of resources. In addition to the integration with the hospital organization through AI, we will allow for efficient use of the overall facility, reducing costs associated with facility and resource utilization, personnel and operator costs, length of stay, and recovery time of the patient, product quality, and rejected batches due to poor product quality. Demonstration of cost-effectiveness will be one core output from our project. Effectiveness of any novel service models in providing economic justification for scale-up investment For smart hospitals to create, deliver

During the preparation period of our BIOMAC OITB and considering the travel limitations due to the COVID19 outbreak, we participated in weekly hangout meetings which were organized for the better planning of the interconnected and multidisciplinary proposal tasks. Our representatives (~80 close to 90 weekly) participated in web meetings (using Hangouts application), contributing through fruitful discussions, updates on the content of our proposal, and further developments to be included in the context of our proposal. Further to this, separate individual group calls among us took place to better facilitate the different aspects related to the Pilot Line Clusters, the services Hubs, the definition of Test Cases, the administrative and operational setup of the BIOMAC ecosystem, as well as other issues regarding the workflows and other organizational matters.

Short Name AUTH

Partner Description  
Description of our organization  
The Aristotle University of Thessaloniki (AUTH) is an academic Institution which is composed of 11 faculties consisting of 36 Schools and involves a critical mass of Faculty approximately equal to 40000 active students (31000 undergraduate and 9,000 postgraduate of which 3952 at doctorate level) and 2366 faculty members. AUTH is the largest University in Greece and in the Balkans and in terms of promoting innovation, 32 patents have been granted during the last 3 years on a national, European and international level. Based on recent data from international ranking lists, of 20372 higher institutions in the world, AUTH from the 407th position in 2011 moved to the 158th in 2012, ranking first among 23 Greek Universities. AUTH has a funding (2009 -2013) (nationally and internationally) for the three major faculties: Health, Sciences and Engineering of a total of 138,330,059 million euros, with the faculty of science having 38,153,623 million euros. It is also worth mentioning that AUTH has 864 international partners and 2355 active research programs (year 2013) and shows an enhancement in the last years not only in funding but also in international cooperation. The School of Chemistry (Faculty of Sciences) was established at AUTH in 1927. From the School of Chemistry, an undergraduate programme can lead to different specializations of i) theoretical chemistry and chemical education, ii) chemical analysis-environmental chemistry-electrochemistry, iii) chemical synthesis-biochemistry and bioapplications, and iv) chemical technology and industrial chemistry. The school has also 11 postgraduate studies, covering the area of chemistry and vast areas of other sciences. The school has 78 faculty members and 10 laboratories. Finally, it has to be noted the EChemTest® which is an electronic self-assessment and assessment tool developed by the European Chemistry Thematic Network Association, aimed at evaluating knowledge and competences in chemical subjects. It has a wide-ranging impact, both formal and informal, in Europe and outside its borders. In our proposal, two Laboratories will be involved, the Laboratory of Chemical and Environmental Technology (LCET) and the Laboratory of Polymer Chemistry and Technology (LPCT). LCET has expertise and infrastructure on thermochemical and (bio)catalytic processes for biomass valorisation to fuels, chemicals, and materials. LPCT focuses on (bio)polymer and (bio)composites synthesis and characterization, as well as applications of (bio)polymers in several areas including pipes, films, bottles, fibers, etc.  

Role in our Project  
PL3 (AUTH, GR): Sugar derived polyols and diols by catalytic hydrogenation/hydrogenolysis  
We will be the coordinator of our project leading WP10 as well as undertaking the scheduled activities described in detail in Section 3. We will have a central role in the management activities of our project and will be responsible for the communication with the European Commission. We will contribute to the BIOMAC project with PL3: Sugar derived polyols and diols by catalytic hydrogenation/hydrogenolysis. Further to this, we have a vital role in WP2 where we will implement the upgrade and modifications of our Pilot Line. Collaborating with other PL owners, we will be involved in Task 3.2 for the Automotive Test Case, producing monomers of polyols (sugars alcohols) and glycols/diols for the value chain of succinate-based polyester and polyurethane resins. Additionally, we will contribute to Task 3.3 that deals with the production of Agricultural Mulch Films Test Case where the glucose-rich streams delivered from PL5 & 6 will be further converted to monomer polyols and diols by us to be used for the production of succinate-based polyesters. Monomer polyols/diols will also be provided by us in Task 3.6, for the Test Case of Printed Electronics. In WP4 (Task 4.2), we will support activities related to the characterization of materials and products. In WP7 we will lead Task 7.3, where the selection of additional test cases will be addressed fitting the Ecosystem capacities. Finally, we will be involved in activities regarding the exploitation and dissemination of the BIOMAC ecosystem in the context of WP8 & 9.

1.1. Objectives and Ambition  
1.1.1. Motivation and Vision  
We developed the Sustainable Products Initiative (SPI) in collaboration with the EC, widening the Ecodesign Directive to reach Green Deal (GD) objectives, leading to sustainable products and making the European market and industry more resource-efficient, competitive, resilient, and innovative. We consider a wide range of products, prioritized by CEAP (e.g., electronics, ICT, textiles, furniture, steel, cement, chemicals) to improve their durability, reusability, upgradability, and reparability, address hazardous chemicals presence, increase energy and resource efficiency, and establish sustainability and circularity. Lack of transparency and traceability of products, their components, substances, and environmental impacts represent a barrier for implementing circular economy business models. Supply chains involving diverse actors (e.g., materials suppliers, producers, recyclers, consumers) complicate information management and the combination of all the different needs and technical capabilities of companies across the value chain. Recently, the 2030 Digital Compass highlighted the importance of digital solutions for achieving GD objectives. The uptake of digital technologies and schemes like the Digital Product Passport (DPP) enables sharing of product-related data across the product lifecycle and along value chains, by tagging, tracing, and localizing down to item-level identification. Several digital technologies (e.g., blockchain, big data, artificial intelligence, digital twins) are proposed for traceability of components and products along value chains, and they are considered key enablers promoting circularity, increasing the inherent recyclability of materials, and upgrading secondary resources, in terms of manifesting their composition as potential secondary raw materials in the same or different process. Enhanced tracing via DigInTraCE aims at the development of i) a transparent and interoperable Decentralized Traceability platform using novel tracking, sensing, and sorting techniques ii) Dynamically updated DPP schemes supporting certification and quality validation iii) AI-based decision-making mechanisms for process and lifecycle optimization iv) upcycling, reuse, and upgrade technologies for improved secondary raw materials use v) contributing to standardization, open and easily accessible data vi) business models creating new economic opportunities and learning resources for employees, promoting new digital skills and meeting regional social needs. We will develop the solutions at TRL6 by the end of our project and demonstrate them in the sectors of a) Pulp & Paper focused on 2 cases: composite wood and furniture; and wood and Pulp & Paper and b) Chemicals focused on 2 cases: plastic parts for ICT equipment and the automotive market; and polymers & textiles. Digital tools combined with novel sensing and sorting techniques are considered an imperative success factor for upgrading secondary resources, enabling cleaner production and recycling, ensuring toxic-free material cycles limiting substances of concern, and producing informed consumers regarding the origin and the way materials are processed. Industrial sectors with high digital traceability needs, as highlighted by the P4P roadmap, include chemicals, pulp and paper (along with the whole forest-based industry and wood value chain), ceramics, and cement. DPP, as an emerging concept for digitally recording product and process information to cover the entire product life cycle by focusing mainly on item-level identification, although being a quite challenging endeavor, is required for enabling circular economy adoption, scaling, and practical implementation, as it provides an easily accessible, centralized registry of diverse data. A physical link (tagging such as QR code) between the product and the data is utilized, allowing value chain stakeholders, end-users, and authorities to directly access a unique digital profile with quantitative/qualitative, static/dynamic, standardized, and machine-readable data related to the product’s origin, composition, repair and dismantling possibilities, end-of-life handling, etc. The implementation of DPP requires multiple data sources supported by existing tools (such as EPREL database, EU eco-labels, existing BOMs, etc.) via a step-by-step and flexible approach, based on information already available and regulated while DPP is used as a link to decentralized information databases. Our methodology and results are aligned with Processes4Planet (P4P) roadmap, GD, CEAP, Digital Single Market, and 2030 Digital Compass objectives and the guidelines of Orgalim, EIT RawMaterials, CEN & CENELEC, and SPI.  
1.1.2. Objectives and Related KPIs  
We will pursue the realization of DigInTraCE through 6 SMART objectives described below:  
O.1: To design, develop, and implement solutions fostering and optimizing the use of secondary raw materials and reducing waste as part of circular value chains. We will assure the circularity of processes of sectors belonging to the process industry by enhancing digitalization (WP3, WP4), upgrading by-products, and optimizing processes, with a special focus on secondary materials, but in the context of the whole value chain (WP2, WP5). We aim to establish a Decentralized Traceability Platform (based on blockchain) applied to holistically optimized circular value chains, assuring availability and access to reliable information enhancing cross-sectoral collaboration (WP2, WP3, WP4). We will produce an advanced transparent information system with the central point being the DPP, supported by a live update mechanism (T2.2, T2.3, T4.1), enhancing the use of secondary raw materials of our own industry and improving cross-sectoral collaboration. Overall, we will comprehensively approach the optimization of secondary materials use through the application of horizontal tracing systems combined with demo/case-specific chemical (e.g., protein recovery process innovation, bio-based adhesive development - T5.2) and digital (data-driven algorithms - T5.3,5.5 together with sensing - T3.1 and sorting - T3.2) processes innovation leading to waste reduction and lower emissions. Relation to the call: “Propose new solutions for improved use of secondary raw materials along the value chain of our own industry or in other industries.”  
KPI O1.1: At least 6 examples (products or process-related) of upcycling, reuse, and upgrade technologies of secondary raw materials implemented.  
KPI O1.2: At least 4 AI-based models developed for secondary raw material optimization processes.  
O.2: To develop and demonstrate new concepts for material tracing while offering a digital decentralized platform allowing tracing and certification of secondary raw materials. We will develop information systems and digital tools for material tracing (WP4) integrated into a multifunctional and interoperable decentralized traceability platform (T4.6) with automatic and real-time updates (T4.1) of information on material composition, quality, history, and other crucial data updated along the value chain, which will be defined through the integration of existing regulations and specific identified needs (T2.2). Those new concepts will be supported by new standardization and certification schemes (T7.1) integrated into digital solutions (T4.5). Standardization deliverables for materials and technologies as well as certification for subproduct types and intended use will contribute to better acceptance of the solutions and efficient launching to the market through support of the transparent and digitalized information system. Through the incorporation of our project findings into new or revised standardization deliverables, that will be available for the industry, the outcomes of our project will be long-lasting. To assure applicability of the concepts in real-life settings, we will apply and demonstrate solutions in 4 pilots representing crucial sectors of i) pulp & paper and ii) chemicals and plastics. Relation to the call: “Propose methodologies for digital tracing and certification of secondary raw materials; Proposals should contribute to standardization, wherever possible.”  
KPI O2.1: Additional Services developed in WP3, WP4 integrated into the Decentralized Traceability platform by M46.  
KPI O2.2: Decentralized traceability platform being developed at TRL6 by M48, having at least 20 active users.  
KPI O2.3: At least 3 certification proposals produced for secondary raw materials based on the analyzed value chains.  
O.3: To design and deliver novel near real-time sensing and sorting mechanisms enhancing data exchange through a dynamic Digital Product Passport. We are basing DigInTraCE on a dynamic DPP scheme indicating properties and history of the products and materials on real closed-loop value chains optimized in terms of waste, by-products, and secondary raw materials with technologies using and supporting computer vision and HIS & LIBS, AI and Machine Learning, robotic sorting, and recovery processes based on novel extraction methods (microwave and/or ultrasonic assisted). Novel real-time sensing and sorting mechanisms will contribute to better identification, characterization, quantification, and separation of by-products of the value chains (T3.1, T3.2, T5.3, T5.5). The collected data dynamically included in the DPP will allow us to trace all produced materials (T4.1). The integration of the existing PolyCertEurope certification scheme will be examined, and novel certification schemes will be defined for subproduct types (T7.1). Relation to the call: “This should include real-time access to information on material compositions and material quality along the whole value chain; Propose digital tools for integration of product passport and/or certification schemes;”  
KPI O3.1: 2 sensing technologies and at least 2 sorting mechanisms deployed at least 3 demonstrator sites.  
KPI O3.2: At least 5 material parameters are added in DPP and continuously updated along the value chain.

1. EXCELLENCE 

1.1 OBJECTIVES SUMMARY 

Foreign body presence in foods is among the biggest sources of complaint and prosecution in the food industry. To us, the food processor or manufacturer, foreign body contamination can be costly as a result of product recalls, legal expenses, and ultimately, the costs of lost reputation and consumer confidence. There is a growing demand for affordable and reliable foreign body detection technology that is fast enough for its in-process use in modern food processing plants. Through our SME Instrument project, we, a Spanish advanced engineering company specializing in online process monitoring and control systems, aim to accelerate the final stages of development and gear up for the commercialization of a new hyperspectral imaging (HSI) system (HYPERA®) for the cost-effective online detection of foreign bodies and chemical composition analysis that will be targeted to the global food industry. Thanks to its novel and disruptive combination of Shortwave Infrared Hyperspectral Imaging (SWIR-HSI) in reflectance with Near Infrared Multispectral Imaging (NIR-MSI) in reflectance/transmittance, our innovative HYPERA system (Figure 1), which has been awarded ‘Seal of Excellence’ certification from the European Commission (see Annex 1), will differentiate itself in the market for its high resolution, its ability to penetrate foodstuffs, its composition differentiation, its compact design and affordability, and its high speeds and versatility. The system will be deployed with its own software package that will contain specialized chemometric tools, to offer the food industry a turnkey solution that is readily customizable for foreign body detection of a range of contaminants, in a wide variety of matrixes and suitable for integration in multiple modern food processing environments. Building on a prototype system (see figure 2 below), which has been validated to TRL6 as a forerunner to our project, over the course of the Phase 2 SME Instruments, we will develop four application cases in foreign body detection in pizzas, ready-to-eat (RTE) foods, processed fruit and vegetables, and cooked meats, whereby the imaging systems will be integrated into rugged system hardware and the software and chemometrics will be further developed, installed, and trialed in processing lines in the industry in order to demonstrate their correct functioning in an operational environment at pre-commercial scale. Positive results from this industry demonstration work will enable us to translate the prototype to a first commercial system and to ensure that manufacturing issues are solved by the end of our project.

In the immediate post-project phase, we will fine-tune as necessary to a full commercial application ensuring that the technology is available for consumers by January 2019. In parallel with the product and application development work, we will also prepare for the commercialization phase to deliver on a business plan, whereby our commercial targets are to sell 500 units in the first 5 years of market entry, leading to a cumulative turnover of €46M for that period, and 42 new jobs being created in IRIS. 

SPECIFIC OBJECTIVES OF OUR PROJECT 

With a view to having near commercial HYPERA® systems for affordable, rapid, and non-destructive foreign body detection in foods by the end of this Phase 2 SME Instrument project, we will fulfill the following objectives during our 24-month work plan: 

1. To develop the overall system specifications and to establish the KPIs for validating the system (Linked to WP1) 

We will systematically establish the user requirements of our collaborating early adopters so that detailed system specifications can be drawn up: 1) CASA TARRADELLAS, a leading Spanish pizza manufacturer; 2) PLATOS TRADICIONALES, an important Spanish producer of Ready-to-Eat (RTE) foods such as lasagne, rice dishes, and roast dishes (supplying MERCADONA, one of the leading retail chains in Spain); 3) MARÍN GIMENEZ, an important Spanish fruit and vegetable processor; and 4) DAWN FARM FOODS from Ireland, a leading European supplier of cooked and fermented meat ingredients to the foodservice and manufacturing sectors, as well as a wider group of food processors. A key aspect of this process is the early implementation of a pilot test with existing prototype equipment to assist the identification of key performance goals. Foreign body detection success criteria should be carefully defined on the basis of considerations that take into account their probabilistic nature, which is governed by the Poisson distribution whereby foreign body occurrence is essentially a “rare event” if the production process has been designed following a HACCP analysis. 

Performance Indicator(s): By M3, we will define the system specifications and establish the KPIs for determining the success of the developed system. 

2. To develop the chemometric predictive models for each application (Linked to WP2) 

The aim is to use the new powerful data processing tools that we will implement based on the most recent advancements in chemometrics for the high speed and multifunctional chemical analysis of the data coming from the sensors and for minimizing the image distortion caused by scattering. In fact, derived from early developments for imaging through both irregular atmospheric paths and biological tissues (medical diagnosis), a variety of non-invasive techniques are currently available for overcoming blurred outputs by means of purely mathematical approaches. We will merge such available mathematical tools with chemometric routines in order to simultaneously attain spatial and chemical information regardless of the scattering due to the nature of the medium. Using the feasibility prototype system in IRIS, we will perform initial tests with product sample sets from our four beta testing clients to obtain test data for the development project and for model development. The models relating spectral data will be validated in the laboratory with selected chemical, physical, and microbiological parameters in selected food matrixes and against available detection methods following traditional (such as artificial vision in the VIS range) and laboratory (i.e., destructive inspection scheme) methods. An initial set of lab-scale ready-to-use predictive chemometric models will be developed, validated, and pre-installed in the respective HYPERA units to be validated. Novel chemometric methods (MCR - multivariate curve resolution- and MLR –multivariate linear regression - improved with wavelengths selection pre-treatment –genetic algorithms- as well as robust classification methods like SVM –support vector machines -) will be implemented for improving accuracy in relation to standard methods like PLS or PLS-DA, for example. 

Performance indicator: By M7, we will have digital files for the first versions of each model ready for laboratory validation and by M9, optimized versions will be delivered for coding into the system software. 

3. To design and build the instrumentation hardware and software prototype (Linked to WP3) 

The Shortwave Infrared Hyperspectral Imaging (SWIR-HSI) in reflectance with Near Infrared Multispectral Imaging (NIR-MSI) in reflectance/transmittance will be scaled up and combined testing performed to verify the effectiveness of the novel combination for offering penetration (NIR) in foodstuffs and for composition differentiation (SWIR) in the target application cases. EXCELLENCE 1.1 OBJECTIVES INTRODUCTION TO THE BUSINESS NEED Over the past 5 to 10 years we have seen important advances in Infrared (IR) spectroscopy technology, with the arrival on the market of low-cost, miniature, fit-for-purpose sensors that can replace bulky systems in R&D, in the field, and in manufacturing environments. While such sensor advancements are undoubtedly helping to increase industry use of NIR-based systems for in process monitoring, the key to unlocking the floodgates for mainstream adoption lies in facilitating the successful integration of IR sensors into processing lines and seamlessly providing the acquired process data to industry in the form of knowledge or intelligence. Let’s consider first of all the hardware integration. This is not just general engineering; this is specialised optical engineering and proper sampling . Sampling is key to the ability to take accurate readings from the material being processed and basically means optimising the interaction between light and sample in order to dr aw relevant information free of interferences and a rtefacts. If the sampling fails, you have fallen at the first hurdle irrespective of the performance of the spectrophotometer. There are no general solutions for sampling; cost- effective trade-offs cannot reliably tackle all the practical cases unless the sampling devices are being prepared by-design to be adapted to a board variety of practical cases. And then there’s the whole data side of things. Robust mathematical tools (i.e. chemometric modelling) are absolutely vital for converting readings into accurate measurements . While a major advantage of NIR spectroscopy techn ology is its sensitivity to the sample matrix , this same advantage makes NIR difficult to work with in that measurements are affected by changes in the sample matrix related to particle size, density, humidity, and temperature. Moreover, if we consider the context of food matrices and pharmaceutical excipients, these can be affected by seasonal, geographical, an d other sources of change that can result in differ ences in the physicochemical compositions of the material, which can have a direct impact in the NIR spectral features. Critically, the development of algorithms based on an understanding of the absorption and scatterin g behaviours of diffuse reflectance and transmittan ce can enable the removal of matrix effects and improved model robustness. The market reality is that today there are not so m any players who are specialising in the development and customisation of fully integrated and customised in-process monitoring solutions and that can deal with the installation challenges that the technology is facing, such calibration of the optical device, addressing fouling, the influence of external factors affecting the system such as temperature, etc.; we tend to have instrument providers on the one side and manufacturers and processors on the other. So there is a clear ‘integration gap’ . Industries willing to take the step to shift thei r laboratory analysis to the line (and this step in volves an almost philosophical and cultural shift in the company, as opposed to merely a technological investment), cannot reasonably do so without a reliable integration, customisation and maintenance service that. A typical food or pharmaceutical manufacturer (irrespective of their size) will not be equipped with the in-house engineering and programming team to get NIR spectroscopy sensors reliably integrated and deployed in their processing lines. In fact, there is a general lack of availability of engineers and scientists with su fficient expertise working with spectroscopy. Consequently, companies that want to use NIR cannot find qualified people to help them do it. Our vision through the VISUM® platform is to bridge this integration gap . Our game changing flexible hardware and software (figure 1 below, and the architecture is described in Table 4 in Section 1.3) equips engineers that do not require a specialization in spectroscopy in the food, pharmaceutical and process industries to perform the rapid, easy, and cost-effective integration of robust, bespoke solutions that are reliable and stable in manufacturing and processing environments. The VISUM® platform is based on an ‘integrated-by-design’ approach that has been born out of a mission to unblock the bottlenecks of the cost and complexity of integration of IR-based ‘mini-PAT’ solutions in processing lines. PROBLEM OR BUSINESS OPPORTUNITY AND HOW THE SOLUTIO N SOLVES THE PROBLEM OR AVAILS OF THE BUSINESS OPPORTUNITY In line with the Industry 4.0 paradigm, there is in creasing industry interest in shifting from laborat ory-based or post-production quality SMEINST-07-2016-2017 6 | P a g e control to in-line monitoring of product and proces s parameters during processing. The main drivers fo r this interest are the need to improve product quality (or to obtain standardised quality) and safety, to increase process efficienci es, to reduce waste and off spec product discard , as well as increasing awareness of the imperative of ICT adoption to remain competitive in our new Digital data-driven era . There is an enormous business opportunity for pro viding even small and mid-tier enterprises with acc ess to rapid and reliable in-process detection techniques that can keep pace with modern processing speeds an d throughputs and which are suitable for: (i) assembly in the production line a nd operating under a realistic environment; (ii) en abling early detection of possible failures; (iii) performing permanent monitoring of the conditions; (iv) providing an assessment of con ditions at any desired time 1. The introduction of spectroscopic sensors for real-time process monitoring is enabling industry to gradually move from inferential monitoring and control toward continuous measurement of core quality parameters 2. VISUM, in line with the objectives of H2020’s Leade rship in Enabling Technologies (LEIT) work programm e, leverages photonics and ICT KETs (Key Enabling Technologies) to drive forwa rd the PAT (Process Analytical Technologies) paradigm within the wider Industry 4.0 framework for improved industrial competitiveness. Over the past 30 years, on/in-line near infrared spectroscopy (NIRS) (based on the absorption of electromagnetic radiat ion at wavelengths in the range 780–2500 nm) has proven to be one of the most efficient and advanced tools fo r continuous monitoring and control of process and product quality in the food processing industry. 3 NIR spectra of foods and pharmaceuticals comprise broad bands arising from overlapping absorptions corresponding mainly to overtones and combinations of vibrational modes involving C–H, O–H, and N–H chemical bonds 4, making it very feasible for measurements to be ma de in organic and biological systems such as meat, fish, fruit and vegetables, grain and grain products, dairy pro ducts, oils, beverages, Active Pharmaceutical Ingre dients (APIs) and excipients, among others. However, despite the operating cost of NIRS being low, to date the instruments themselves have been costly , which has limited practical application 3. Moreover, calibration models based on NIR spectroscopy, and especially for on-line applications, tend to require a significant economical effort and are not reliable and stable enough when used practically 3. Our VISUM platform breaks down the cost and complex ity barriers of NIR technology adoption for real-ti me process monitoring applications, by providing food and pharmaceutical processors (irrespective of their size or technolog ical capability) with access to the necessary flexible hardware and software to allow f or rapid, easy, and cost-effective integration of r obust, customised IR solutions that are reliable and stable in manufacturing and proces sing environments. The aim is to ensure that integr ation can be performed even by non-specialised engineers. This is a timely develop ment in view of the emergence of new compact, highl y affordable IR spectroscopy sensors, such as those that are offered by Finnish technology company, Spectral Engines OY, which can be purchased for <€1k. Their business models are centred selling high volumes of sensors. The key now to mass adoption lies in faci litating the integration of these sensors in industry. (It is important to highlight that since we have commenced the VISUM development we have witnessed dramatic price drops for IR sensors from €5/6 to just under €1k- for orders of 10 units) The VISUM platform will capitalise on this business opportunity by bringing to market the first IR spectroscopy integration platform that offers the following Unique Selling Propositions (USPs):  Affordability- the use of the new wave of extremely affordable MEMS-NIR sensors to enable disruptive cost reductions (~up to 4- 5 times cheaper) for fully customised in-process monitoring systems.  Ease of integration and reliability- In relation to the reliability of the calibration m odels, the VISUM platform will incorporate a robust multivariate-models development environment and a clever ‘calibration transfer program’, thus eliminating the need for a full recalibration. This will be a fundamental feature i n making the VISUM platform readily customisable fo r reliable use in many analytical measurement scenarios in industry.  Low maintenance- A major practical problem that continues to pose great challenges in relation to in-line spectroscopy is biofouling and contamination of the optical window, caused by the environment in which the device is operating. V ISUM offers two solutions (depending on the requirements of the application) for ensuring resistance to fouling- and thereby inc reasing the robustness and reliability of the device and dramatically reducing the maintenance of the hardware and extending its useful life-: a) a layer of specific polymers (non-polar compounds) coated on the surface of the optical window (tests during our Phase 1 Feasibility Study revealed positive results 5) for preventing fouling. The work packages with cor responding milestones and deliverables are described in detail in table 3.1a. Milestones are designed as the point w here the first available information from the WP will be handed over to the MyToolBox e-platform for implementation. Th e content and usability of this information will be assessed by key -users during the project and finally fine-tuned in the last phase of WP3. SFS-13-2015 MyToolBox 33 Figure 3: The MYTOOLBOX Work Packages In close cooperation with the end -users in our consortium, crop yield and mycotoxin contaminat ion after current crop management will be compared with crop yields and mycotoxin contamination after using the novel pre-harvest interventions (WP1). These include investigation of genetic resistance to fungal infection, cultural control, the use of novel biopesticides (organic-farming compliant), and competitive biocontrol treatment. Field trials will be carried out in North-West and South Europe and the results will be translated into clear and easy to use management strategies included in MyToolBox. Research into post-harvest measures (WP2), including real-time monitoring during storage to be carried out in 3 countries including China , innovative sorting of crops, novel milling technology and efficient biofuel production, will enable raw cereal materials with higher mycotoxin levels to be processed without breaching regulatory limits on mycotoxins in finished products. To overcome problems in a relation to user acceptance of the web -based MyToolBox decision support e-platform, our multi -actor approach and well managed innovation cycle ensures that stakeholder groups are involved at all stages of the development of MyToolBox e-platform (WP3) to ensure that their feedback is considered in the iterative development approach. Their involvement will be crucial to translate the key components of HACCP, GAP and the Decision Support System (DSS) for pre - and post-harvest treatments together with legal aspects and socio-economic drivers of the actors into clear, unambiguous advices to prevent mycotoxin contamination and the safe use of contaminated batches. Hence, the individual measures will be implemented in the MyToolBox decision support e-platform (WP4), followed by the evaluation of the tool from various angles (mostly face-to-face interviews with key-users and use of questionnaires) through a multi -actor approach with end users and stakeholder groups at relevant points in the food chain in different countries, and this assessment will be conducted feeding SFS-13-2015 MyToolBox 34 outputs into WP4 to aid the socio -economic analysis. The cost-effectiveness of the developed intervention strategies in combination with the MyToolBox e -platform to effectively monitor and reduce the incidence of mycotoxin contaminants in crop production and in the food and feed supply chains will be verifie d a) for Fusarium mycotoxins in wheat in the UK and b) for aflatoxins in maize in Serbia and c) in cooperation with the Chinese partner, for the peanut supply chain in China. Early in the project, a business plan will be developed in order to draw up a robust business model for the commercial exploitation (WP5) and maintenance of the MyToolBox outcomes after the project: this is the key to the sustainability of the measures developed in the project and of its web-based MyToolBox platform and it will be centred on a strong value proposition to the end user community. Within the MyToolBox project we will also implement a communication campaign including training (WP5) to reach the targeted stakeholders including farmers and their associations and to disseminate information about the project and its results to actors and stakeholders along the food supply chain, to opinion leaders/regulators, the media, professionals working in agriculture and food manufacture, educators, and other multiplier s, food and drink industries (including SMEs), consumer organisations, the scientific community and the general public. A compliance assessment will be carried out to identify critical issues in legislation/standards in order to implement MyToolBox and to anticipate legislative implications of the project outputs. Project management (WP6) will closely follow the actions and developments initiated by all partners with the aim to reach all objectives within the time and budget limits. Description of work (broken down into tasks), and role of participants T1.1 Alternative control (incl. control suitable for organic farming) of Fusarium mycotoxins in small grain cereals (M1-M40) Lead: HAU PM; Partners: BOKU 4.5PM; BIOFORSK 3.5PM; AXEB 1.25PM; AGRO LV 0.8PM; HAU and BIOFORSK will conduct field experiments in the UK and Norway on the reduction of mycotoxins in small grain cereals through the application of biopesticide technology. Current chemical control of Fusarium mycotoxins is limited to a single chemical group, the triazoles. This creates a large selective pressure for fungicide resistance to develop and there are currently safety concerns with triazole fungicides as they are classed as endocrine disruptors, and as such they may be removed from the market. This task will look at a number of alternatives to triazole fungicides. This will include alternative chemistry as well as novel biopesticides such as salts, botanical extracts, plant defence elicitors and biocon trol agents. Several recent studies conducted at HAU, BIOFORSK and other institutes have shown reductions using various forms of biopesticides, for example preliminary experiments at BIOFORSK have shown significant reduction in Fusarium and its mycotoxins in wheat after treatment with a foliar fertiliser. This study will allow a comprehensive comparison of different categories of biopesticide s to be evaluated within the same experiments. Due to their nature several of these alternatives are suitable for org anic as well as conventional production systems. An identical matrix of up to 18 biopesticides will be tested on wheat in the UK and on oats in Norway in comparison to untreated and triazole (prothioconazole) treated controls in year 1. In the growing seasons of years 2 and 3 a selection of biopesticides showing most promising activity will be optimised for dose and timing. HAU will use artificial inoculation with Fusarium graminearum and mist irrigation t o generate high disease pressure whilst BIOFORSK plan to use natural infection at sites with high disease pressure. Both institutes have used these strategies successfully for many years to test the efficacy of products against Fusarium infection and subsequent mycotoxin contamination of harvested grain s. Field experiments will be conducted according to EPPO guidelines . In order to verify the the efficacy of the plant protection products , the harvested grains will be quantified for deoxynivalenol using immunoassay (ELISA) technology by HAU and BIOFORSK. In addition, and to verify the impact of these biopesticides on mycotoxins beyond Deoxynivalenol, targeted analysis of all relevant Fusarium mycotoxins including their masked (conjugated) forms will be carried out by BOKU using a sophisticated and fully validated LC-MS/MS based multi-analyte method, which covers more >300 secondary metabolites of fungi. BOKU will also ensure accuracy and comparability of the ELISA measurement results through LC -MS/MS reference analyses and by pro viding laboratory- and certified reference materials. In 54 Partner underlined is WP leader SFS-13-2015 MyToolBox 37 Europe, EPPO guidelines now constitute a reference in Commission Regulation (EU) No 545/2011 of 10 June 2011, implementing Regulation (EC) No 1107/2009 regarding the data requirements for plant prote ction products. AXEB will advise on the biopesticide technology and take forward the commercialisation of potential biopesticide products (see also WP5). AGRO LV will advise on end -user suitability of the biopesticide technology and conduct demonstration trails of successful products for growers in year 4. T1.2 Cultural control of Fusarium mycotoxins in small grain cereals (M1-M40) Lead: HAU 55PM; Partners: BOKU 4.5PM; BIOFORSK 9PM; AGRO LV 0.2PM; Cultural control mechanisms will be investigated focusing on the study of the crop debris management in this Task. This will include microbial agents to colonise and exclude Fusarium inoculum from plant debris and the use of biofumigation crops. These biol ogical control strategies are suitable for organic as well as conventional production systems. BIOFORSK will conduct laboratory and field studies on the efficacy of chemical and biological control agents to reduce the inoculum of Fusarium in crop debris fo r oats in Norway. This will involve an initial screen of up to 20 products in laboratory studies followed by optimisation and validation of selected products from year 1 in field experiments in the growing seasons in year 2 and 3. Preliminary experiments at BIOFORSK have shown significant reduction in Fusarium inoculum after treatment with Biowish (fertiliser that stimulates microbial activity) and some reduction by Trifender ( Trichoderma asperellum). HAU will conduct studies in glasshouse on the efficacy o f biofumigation technology to reduce Fusarium inoculum in crop debris followed by optimisation and validation in the growing seasons in years 2 and 3 for wheat and maize rotations in the UK. Within this task Fusarium inoculum will be quantified using qPCR technology developed and validated in previous projects at HAU and BIOFORSK. AGRO LV will advise on the end user suitability of debris management technology . As described in T1.1, targeted analysis of the Fusarium mycotoxins will be performed by a novel and fully validated LC- MS/MS based multi -analyte method developed by BOKU, which covers all relevant myocotoxins including their masked (conjugated) forms. T1.3 Reduction of aflatoxins in maize through genetics (drought resistance, early maturity) (M1-M40) Lead: UNS 15.6PM; Partners: BOKU: 0.5 PM; AGROCENTRUM 4PM; HORTA 11.5PM UNS and HORTA will conduct field experiments in Serbia and Italy to determine the resistance of local and European maize hybrids to aflatoxin contamination at harvest. A range of maize hybrids will be screened for aflatoxin resistance based on known genetic traits that impact on aflatoxin contamination (namely drought tolerance and rate of maturity). Hybrids will be selected based on local and European origin as well as the screening of newly developed hybrids. An initial screen will be conducted of 50 genotypes with a single sowing date and location (Serbia) in year 1. In the growing seasons in year 2 and 3, 20 selected hybrids will be tested at 2 locations (Serbia and Italy) and with 2 sowing dates (early and late). Experiments will be conducted according to EPPO guidelines and harvested grains will be quantified for aflatoxin using ELISA tests. BOKU will ensure accuracy and comparability of measurement results through complementary LC-MS/MS reference analyses and by providing laboratory- and certified reference materials. In addition to the individual farmers involved through Agrocentrum the results of the field trials for ear rot resistance will be presented at the annual meeting organised by Agrocentrum (´field day´) for breeding companies, agricultural organisations and grain producers, to obtain feedback from end users and other relevant agricultural institutions. T1.4 Biocontrol for aflatoxins in maize (M1-M40) Lead: UNS 36.4PM; Partners: BOKU 0.5 PM; AGROCENTRUM 8PM; CAAS-IAPPST 2PM Local (Serbian) atoxigenic isolates of Aspergillus flavus have been identified and will be tested within field experiments. UNS and AGROCENTRUM will conduct studies of 2 atoxigenic isolates on 20 maiz e hybrids in 12 field experiments (6 non-irrigated and 6 irrigated experiments) split between 2 sites and conducted in years 1 -3. Experiments will be conducted according to EPPO guidelines and harvested grains will be quantified for aflatoxin using ELISA t esting. BOKU will ensure accuracy and comparability of the measurement results through the measures described in T1.1 und T1.3. T ask 1.4 will be a novel adaptation within Europe of proven technology developed in America, and currently being adopted in Afri ca and Asia, aided by the collaboration of international experts from those continents (Dr Peter Cotty, USDA and member of the MyToolBox Advisory Board ; Dr Martin Kimanya, The Nelson Mandela African Institution of Science and Technology; Prof Liu Yang, CAA S-IAPPST). In particular Prof Liu Yang will provide detailed information on the production, formulation, application, standardisation and long -term monitoring of biocontrol agents within crops based on on -going projects within China. AGROCENTRUM will advise on enduser suitability of the maize hybrids and atoxigenic biocontrol strategies as well as demonstrate experiments to growers. Description of work (broken down into tasks), and role of participants T2.1 Real time monitoring systems for improving grain storage management solutions for mycotoxins in stored cereals and nuts T2.1.1 Design and testing of measurement device for real -time monitoring of key abiotic parameters using ATEX compliant sensor platform components (M1-M18) Lead: IRIS Partners: CRANFIELD ; BARILLA ASAG The designs of the measurement device will be drawn up. The device will include an IR CO2 sensor, a Temperature (T) and Relative Humidity (RH) sensor. To this end, we will optimise the range of infra-red (IR) CO2 sensor detection required in different grain types: studies will be conducted with the CO 2 sensor platform to identify the range of concentrations which need to be measured for wheat/barley, oats and peanuts under different moisture content for real-time monitoring in farm stores and grain silos depending on relative grain bulk. In addition, a review of commercially available T and RH sensors will be performed in order to select the most optimal for integration into the sensor node required. The sensor node (into which the sensor components will be integrated) will be designed, as will the specifications for its encapsulation in order to ensure ruggedness, water and air tightness. Each n ode will be coded with the source code for its intercommunication ensuring no noise and interference with other systems, low consumption and seamless communication at all times. It will be ensured that the node can accurately monitor in the range required for different grain silo sizes. During the design stage we will ensure that we fufill all the criteria for ATEX compliance based on an “ATEX-by-Design” approach. The integrated CO 2/RH/T sensor platform will be tested in real grain silo conditions . The num ber of sensor nodes required to adequately cover a standard commercial silo will be studied. BARILLA and ASAG (China) will SFS-13-2015 MyToolBox 39 provide input with regard to the number of sensors and placement considerations for later practical trials. The fabricated nodes will then be installed in a pilot scale silo, and in -situ functionality and performance assessment tests will be carried out, in order to verify: (a) the ability to withstand pressures and long term placement in grain stores; the ability to provide rapid, accu rate and repeated readings of CO 2/HR/T; to offer sufficient range to ensure measurements from the whole silo can be obtained; (b) correlation with existing CO 2 respiration results of mycotoxigenic moulds in different commodities which are available from CRANFIELD. Following an analysis of the results of the pilot trials, the necessary modifications and improvements will be made to the sensor platform and designs of an optimised sensor will be drawn up, from which production of the final improved sensor platform will be performed. T2.1.2 Biotic mould spoilage and mycotoxin models (M1-M18) Lead: CRANFIELD Partners: IRIS ; BOKU ; ASAG The work in T2.1.2 is complementary to T2.1.1 as it will provide data on rates of CO 2 production in relation to fungal activity in cereals at different moisture contents and temperatures. This will inform the design and development of the sensor platform and will be combined with the existing knowledge base at CRANFIELD for some mycotoxigenic moulds which is already available. ASAG and CAAS-IAPPST will provide peanut samples and corresponding biological data for these studies. Boundary temperature x moisture content models will be developed for growth and mycotoxin production in cereals/pea nuts specifically for those toxins which are not available: zearalenone in wheat; T2/HT-2 in oats; aflatoxin in peanuts. The temporal production of CO 2 by mycotoxigenic fungi under different good, marginal and poor storage conditions will be obtained and t his will be related to relative potential risks of mycotoxins exceeding the EU legislative limits. Analyses of the various mycotoxins will be carried out by BOKU using a novel LC-MS/MS multi-toxin-method as described in T1.1. T2.1.3 Integration of abiotic data with biological data (M18-M36) Lead: CRANFIELD Partners: IRIS ; BARILLA ; ASAG Real-time abiotic data on CO 2, T and RH from pilot scale silos will be integrated with the biological growth/toxin boundary models using process engineering solutions. This will be done by linking the rates of CO 2 production under different temperature and RH conditions to these boundary models and the relationship to exceeding the EU legislative limits for the toxins in each grain type. The integrated system will include (a) commodity type and (b) toxin of interest. Trials will be run to test the prototype system in small scale bin systems in farm stores and finally in large grain silos (including silos in China, see WP3) for accuracy and for remote downloadi ng by store managers. This will be utilised in the development of the final DSS system tool for post -harvest management that will be developed in WP3. T2.2 Innovative

Excellence 1.1 Objectives Nano-scaled materials are abundant in different stages of industrial manufacturing and increasingly used in a number of applications from biomedical to packaging, automotive or energy. Physical and chemical properties of these materials are strongly dependent on their size, making the characterisation of mean size, size distribution, and shape of nano-scaled particles a major critical aspect for the quality and efficiency of manufacturing processes. Yet, conventional characterisation technologies still display manifold shortcomings which represent a major innovation obstacle for us as manufacturers of nanoparticles. In particular, in most cases, we have to perform these via offline testing of sampled materials, which results in very high characterisation times, impossibility of controlling product quality and reliability during the process, as well as higher costs derived from resource-consuming laboratory procedures. The increasingly demanding market for nano-scaled materials and nanoparticles (NPs) due to the need of guaranteeing product quality and regulatory compliance is considered mandatory for us to stay competitive in the European market and beyond. Consequently, the development of new technologies capable of improving the speed and reliability of nanoparticle characterisation in real-time industrial applications is a major demand for us as producers of nano-scaled materials. The estimated market size for nano-characterisation has been estimated at 573.7 million Euros for 2016, expecting to grow at an expected CAGR of 7.3 % due to the rise of international quality standards for materials1. While focusing on the most representative chemical production of nano-scaled particles in suspensions, our project will bridge this gap using three novel, complementary real-time in situ particle size characterisation technologies – Photon Density Wave spectroscopy (PDW), OptoFluidic force induction (OF2i) and Turbidity spectrometry (TUS). These technologies will be advanced from the “lab-status” (TRL 4) to a technology demonstration level for inline/online2 process monitoring at pilot scale in the industrial environment of our industrial partners. This is accompanied by a simultaneous development of a PAT (Process Analytical Technology) Software Platform for holistic digital process data storage and analysis, in conjunction with process modelling and the development of a Decision Support System (DSS) to fuse the data-rich multi-modal results of the nano-characterisation technologies with the industrial production reality, allowing for a digitalized real-time process and quality control. Having an even broader potential, the validation of our solution will be performed in 5 different complementary application cases in large and small companies for the production of mineral, polymer and conversion of ceramic NPs electrolyte suspensions with different shape and aggregation behaviours. Importantly, our project will deliver a set of recommendations for standards as well as nanosafety activities to demonstrate the advantage of the inline monitoring approach. To support the roll out of our project results in the NPs processing industries, we will also cover technical and transversal training activities also used as a vector for cross-fertilization with other related projects encapsulated in a clustering strategy supported by our involvement in numerous EU networks, like SPIRE, ETPN or NanoSafetyCluster, as well as in synergistic ongoing H2020 projects. Our project includes a suite of relevant stakeholders along the supply and value chain to prepare the successful deployment of NanoPAT. Our consortium consists of 14 members, representing instrument developers, process data and modelling experts, academic process researchers, industrial processing specialists, as well as innovation and dissemination experts. We will intensively contribute to enhancing the innovation capacity of the European nanotechnology sector. We aim at empowering small- to large-scale European industrial manufacturers of nano-scaled particles to gain understanding and thus control on the underlying particle formation processes during production. This will enable us to reduce cost (increased space-time-yield, reduced feedstock and energy demand) and to save on time-to-market for product development as well as to increase product quality and consistency. All in all, the overriding goal of our project is to deliver three novel, real-time nano-characterisation Process Analytical Technologies (PAT) validated in five different industrial manufacturing environments, including real-time data handling for digital production process monitoring and quality control. This implies that innovating PATs integrated inline (PDW, TUS) or online (OF2i) will be paired with new data-analytical methodologies in order to provide, for the first time, a real-time analysis for manufacturing processes of particles in the nanometre scale with sub minute temporal resolution. By overcoming the current challenges linked to NPs characterisation, and validating new versatile technologies for real-time process monitoring in a variety of sectors, as well as thanks to complementary transversal activities, we will have enduring impacts for significantly improving the manufacturing of nanoparticles. To achieve our goals, we have defined a 48-month work plan with the following specific objectives, linked to their specific WPs and related key performance indicators (KPIs), which will be used as milestones (MS) for project implementation. Objective 1: To define the specifications of the processes to be monitored via the tailored NanoPAT technologies (WP1) After an updated state of the art patent and market review, we will outline the industrial requirements for each of the five nanoparticles production and conversion processes involved in NanoPAT with the support of the reference RTOs.
