5 1. Excellence We will develop a world-leading Rapid Ultra-Short Pulse Laser Surface Texturing technology for the functionalisation of a wide range of materials and products. Our consortium consists of EU-leading organisations that cover the full value chain for laser surface engineering, with access to all of the necessary resources to develop the technology and provide access to European and global markets. The 4 end-user partners: MAIER, CRF, ARCELIK and JJVC are all recognised in their industrial sectors: Automotive, white goods and fast-moving consumer goods. In order to minimise the ultimate time to market and to maximise exploitation, we will build the demonstration unit by PRIMA, who are already suppliers of these integrated systems to the European and global markets. By building on our consortium’s existing technologies and developments, we will overcome the existing barriers that are currently limiting developments and holding back ultra-short pulse laser texturing from achieving mainstream application, through developing: • USP laser architectures with pulse duration <10ns, powers >1kW and pulse repetition rates above 500Hz. • Direct Laser Interference Patterning (DLIP) technology to achieve multiple length-scale periodic patterns (3-10µm) with processing speed of 2-5 m²/min. • USP laser architectures of M2 beam quality ≤15 or spatial coherence >15% and coherence length equal to 3mm. • Industrial grade fibre cables that are able to transmit peak power densities above 5GW/cm² over a minimum 3m length. • Optical inspection methods for assessing the quality of the produced functional surface textures at the high production speed (up to several m²/min) and respond via process control. In so doing, our project will transform our current technologies from the current TRL 4 to create a market-leading solution that will have clear advantages over existing solutions and demonstrate those in an industrial relevant environment (TRL 6). We will directly address and contribute to the wider Europe 2020 targets through increased employment of 20-64 years old, increased R&D spending, reduced energy usage and greenhouse emissions, and increased education, especially at third level education as well as the 10 EC priorities for 2015-19 Jobs, growth and investment and the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs). Surface engineering is a critical technology that adds functionality to materials surfaces through texturing, surface modification or coating. Currently, coating solutions dominate due to their higher level of maturity, relatively low cost and higher speed. However, surface coatings have a number of limitations, including: • Limited durability and effectiveness over long-time periods. • Need for potentially harmful chemicals, many of which are regulated through the REACH legislation. • Poor sustainability and resource efficiency due to the chemicals and processes employed. Enhanced surface functionality through surface texturing has existed for a number of years. The earliest commercial application demonstrated on cylinder liner honings i,ii, with more recent applications shown for texturing of magnetic storage devices iii,iv and for MEMS devices for overcoming adhesion and stiction problems v. Early work in the 1960s relating to tribology vi,vii,viii was based on the concept of surface texturing providing micro-reservoirs to enhance lubricant retention or as micro-traps to capture wear debris ix. Current Surface Texturing (LST) methods: • Electron and Ion-Beam lithography for the fabrication of high-resolution surface patterns with small feature sizes down to ~100 nm. However, these techniques are inherently limited to 0.008-0.03 cm²/min x which makes them infeasible for many applications. • Conventional micro machining such as micro-milling can achieve high processing speeds (e.g. ~0.01 m²/min) but at low resolution (e.g. 15-100 μm) which significantly limits the type of achievable surface features. • Chemical etching is also an option but is restricted due to the use of potentially harmful chemicals and other challenges related to scalability and control make it widely unattractive to end users. Figure 1 Fabrication speed vs. Feature size 6 Each of these techniques typically suffers from low productivity, due to the size and complexity of features required for surface functionality. There is an inevitable trade-off between resolution/definition and speed. Laser surface texturing (LST) is a highly promising surface engineering technique that offers: Excellent repeatability; Non-contact processing; The ability of achieving small feature size and the possibility of achieving high quality finishing. Over the last decade there has been a large number of academic publications on using LST for the manufacture of hydrophobic, high friction, oleophilic (for oil trapping) surfaces etc. However, despite the promising results, the industrial uptake has been very limited and in most cases the technology has stalled at TRL 4. The reasons identified for this failure to progress beyond TRL is attributed to: i) High cost, ii) Poor productivity and iii) Lack of knowledge. USP lasers are ideally suited for surface texturing and ablation applications on temperature sensitive materials and substrates. Whilst USP laser processes have been widely applied in industrial applications already, their widespread take up has been limited due to four main technical limitations of current laser surface texturing, including: • Heat accumulation and oxidation • Synchronisation of laser pulses • Plasma Shielding effects • Precision at high processing speeds Our project directly addresses these shortfalls by developing a novel high power 1.5 kW USP laser system, which utilises the average power and repetition rate of the ultrashort pulsed laser source more effectively than current methods via spreading the pulses temporally across the component at industrially relevant speeds, whilst ensuring minimal thermal degradation of the component’s surface. The approach uses a direct laser interference patterning (DLIP) technique that is uniquely suited to high power applications as the increased power is spread across the interference pattern, enabling high power utilisation whilst maintaining high precision from the interference patterns. Current systems are intrinsically limited – as the power is increased, the scan rate must also be increased to prevent melting that results in lower resolution. Increasing scanning rates leads to an inherent loss of resolution and control. This is an innovative solution for high speed surface patterning of periodic structures in a one step process whereby periodic structures can be produced in different materials including metals, ceramics, polymers and coatings in a single step process. This approach will enable our consortium to utilise novel laser texturing methods for superior temperature control and ultra-high processing speeds. The technology has the potential to create functional surfaces with optimal friction, hydrophobic or hydrophilic, anti-corrosion or other properties. Our solution will build on previous research and development work by our consortium to create: Ultra-Short Pulse (USP) lasers from APL with pulse durations in the nanosecond regime down to the femtosecond regime. The average laser power will be increased to power within nanosecond lasers towards 2-3 kW. We will initially start with pulse durations in the range of 5-15 ns and then seek to develop to a few hundred picoseconds. The power may be as much as 25 mJ. This will be combined with an innovative Direct Laser Interference Patterning (DLIP) from FRAUNHOFER. As such a laser system would have to be highly multi-mode (M2~30), a novel M2 transformation beam shaping method based on diffractive optics will be developed and utilised by HOLO-OR to achieve effective interference patterning with a partially coherent source. This approach will build on current similar novel M2 transformation HOLO-OR systems that have been shown to produce good interference patterns. To allow for process control to be achieved, process monitoring and control from VISUM and AIMEN will also be developed. These will be integrated into a demonstrator system consisting of the laser, M2 transformation, beam splitting and fibre delivery that can achieve processing speeds of 2-5 m²/min for a range of materials and geometries. This will enable the technology to be demonstrated for the next generation of manufacturing of functional textured surfaces in a broad range of industrial sectors, including: 1. Automotive – Exterior chrome-plated bumper trim with self-cleaning properties. Almost planar with some curvature. 330 mm x 30 mm x 20 mm. 2. Automotive – laser surface texturing of cylinder piston liners to replace mechanical helical side-honing to deliver controlled surface roughness and reduced piston friction. 3. Automotive – Improved mould tools with low friction surface to eliminate lubricants for aluminium hot forming process. 4. White goods - Hydrophobic texturing for dishwashers and tumble dryer heat exchangers to reduce energy consumption required for drying and condensers. 5. Fast moving consumer goods (FMCG) – oleophobic surface texturing of plastic packaging for FMCG application to reduce current practice of overfilling and to enable the customer to extract maximum product. 6. Health - antibacterial functionality for PROLENE suture (JJVC) and improved cement adhesion on PEEK orthopaedic implants (JJVC) 1.1. Objectives The overall aim of our project is to develop high power ultra-short pulse lasers and the associated optics to enable the precise periodic texturing of surfaces to impart a range of surface functionalities at unprecedented processing speeds. Key Overarching Objectives: Our project has a number of broad qualitative objectives, including; • To manufacture textured functional surfaces utilising fewer raw materials, less energy and less waste. • To improve accuracy, power and control over existing technologies. • To achieve fast materials processing with processing speeds 2-5 m²/min, representing a significant increase on current laser techniques. • To increase achievable precision. • To minimize heat impact on sensitive materials. • To increase productivity. • To increase achievable flexibility and product customization. • Significantly reduce processing costs. 1. Excellence 

1.1. Objectives 

We are uniquely positioned within the European process industry, which represents the economic roots of the European economy by transforming raw materials into intermediate and end-user products while also using energy, water, and possibly producing byproducts, emissions, and wastes. We thus sit at the core of most industrial value chains via discrete manufacturing into a whole host of sectors. Our process industry is characterised by a high dependence on resources (energy, raw materials, and water) in production. Optimising the effectiveness of our process control systems and strategies is vital to improve quality, reliability, and efficiency, as well as the sustainability of our operations. Following the SPIRE philosophy, our project aims to develop an integrated process control approach (figure 1) embedding a novel monitoring system based on low-cost analysers combined with smart sensors for monitoring in real time the critical control parameters of key processes in the minerals, ceramics, non-ferrous metals, and chemical process industries. The data from our sensors and analysers will be seamlessly managed via a versatile Global Control System Platform that will acquire, process, and mine the data for closed-loop control of the process or for decision support. Our ProPAT platform is highly scalable for the integration of numerous analysers in the future, and its flexible database enables data to be combined with information coming from business systems to guarantee structural consistency with the whole process or compatibility with existing supervision systems. 

Figure 1- ProPAT integrated process control approach 

Our ProPAT development will deliver the following key innovations: 
a) Affordable high-end probes (low-cost NIRs and particle size analyser) that will enable their use in several critical points along the same process. 
b) MEMS-based standard sensors (temperature, flow rate, pressure) which may, in the long term, be powered by energy harvesting approaches (battery-less), wirelessly connected to the data logging subsystem. This will open up the possibility for some of them to form part of the process ("flow tracker sensors") in order to report bulk properties that are very difficult to measure by static sensors. 
c) Process-oriented development and validation of our ProPAT platform at several subsequent scales for assisting in the production of minerals, ceramics, non-ferrous metals, and chemicals (plastic and pharmaceuticals). 
d) An integrated PAT platform with tailored hard- and soft-solutions according to the process and applications of interest. Analytical sensors will be combined with data handling tools, including classical MSPC (multivariate statistical process control), process modelling, and data fusion for multisensor interpretation when needed. 

The specific objectives that we will fulfill over the course of our ProPAT project are discussed below (effort has been made to ensure that these objectives are S.M.A.R.T. -Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Realistic, and Timely- and clearly linked to our work plan and milestones). 

1. To perform an analysis of the quality control measures currently available to or implemented by the target mineral, ceramics, non-ferrous metals, and chemical industries (WP1). This analysis will identify the critical control parameters governing variability along the process chain of the target industries (including major processes such as polymerisation, milling, mixing, granulation, drying). We will identify the currently used control strategies. Other available approaches will be researched from the state of the art. Initial recommendations for improvements in process efficiency will be made, while at the same time aiming to improve final product quality, as well as resource and energy efficiency. Variability will be qualified in terms of: raw material variability, in-process variability, final product variability, cycle time variability, product wastage, and energy consumption variability. An assessment of the proposed ProPAT sensors and analysers will be made and discussed with the end-users in terms of integration requirements: sampling requirements, speed of analysis, cleaning-in-place, sensor fusion, etc. Similarly, we will carry out an analysis of requirements in terms of data analytics and integration with current data management systems. Emphasis will be placed on the potential adoption of ‘Green PAT’ strategies to reduce energy and waste during processing due to advanced process control PATs. The required and desired features of a PAT strategy for the target applications will be produced and used to draw up the development and adoption strategies of ProPAT. A concept design and an integration strategy will be established for the different versions of our ProPAT platform to be rolled out in the selected target production lines. 

Performance indicator: By M3, end-user specifications will be defined to enable PAT development work to commence, and by M6, we will have a roadmap in place to effectively guide our efforts in relation to potential PAT strategies for various control parameters along the process chains of the target industries. 

2. To set up and calibrate the novel scatterometer analyser for real-time and online measurements during granulation (WP2). This novel sensor will be capable of measuring particle sizes above a micron, offering a very low cost and rapid response. At the project start, we will integrate the scattometric sensor in a suitable test rig to enable the offline calibration of the sensor vs. standard measurements in the lab using relevant sample sets selected by the end users in the pharma, ceramics, non-ferrous metals, and minerals industries. In parallel, we will optimise a replica sensor for its use in an industrial environment. 

Performance indicator: By M12, the granulometric sensor will be calibrated vs. conventional sensors offline, and by M16, an optimised robust version will be delivered for its integration with the rest of the hardware in WP4. 

3. To set up and improve the novel low-cost MEMS-NIR spectroscopic analyser (WP3). A novel MEMS-NIR spectroscopic analyser will be integrated to measure compositional data at low cost vs. commercial NIR instruments and offering a rapid response. At the project start, we will integrate the NIR sensor in a suitable test rig to enable the offline calibration of the sensor vs. standard measurements in the lab using relevant sample sets selected by the end users in the pharma and chemical industries. In the test rig, we will test and optimise three basic requirements of the NIR sensor: 1) high accuracy to obtain reproducible measurements, useful to develop calibration models or to be fed into other control-oriented data analysis tools, 2) design and housing in order to work in demanding environmental conditions, and 3) inputs for the sampling method that will be developed in WP4 and needs an exact adapted optical-mechanical system. Various techniques will be used depending on the compositional parameters of interest as defined in the state of the art review and feedback from the survey of participating industries in WP1. In parallel, we will optimise IR sensors with different wavelength ranges for their use in an industrial environment so they can be delivered for their integration with the rest of the hardware in WP4. 

Performance indicator: By M19, the different versions of the robust MEMS-NIR sensor will be delivered with adjusted wavelength ranges for each product of interest for their integration with the rest of the hardware in WP4. 

4. To develop chemometric models for individual sensors and methods for analysing multisensor inputs (related to WP3 and 4). Development of software for individual sensors involves those providing a multivariate response (especially, the NIR sensor and, to a lesser extent, the scatterometer). For the NIR sensor, we can envision two main axes: process monitoring and control and process modelling. For the first one, Multivariate Statistical Process Control (MSPC) based on exploratory analysis of spectra can be implemented as well as control of product properties, through suitable multivariate calibration tools based on Partial Least Squares (PLS) or other algorithms when needed. Special attention will be focused on data preprocessing, wavelength selection methods, adequate sampling, and correct upgrading of models from well-controlled laboratory conditions to pilot-plant scale, where an adaptation process (known as model transfer) and new validation will be required. Complementing the process control aspect, NIR sensors could also be equipped with process modelling tools that could provide (when applicable) information on the development of a reaction (in synthesis processes) through the use of hard-modelling tools based on physicochemical models or to the description of the general transformation of a system, e.g., in blending processes, through soft-modelling methodologies, such as Multivariate Curve Resolution (MCR). Hybrid hard-soft methodologies can also be envisioned to deal with non-ideal response behaviours or with systems in which the response variation cannot be fully described by a first principles model. Some multivariate raw data from the scatterometer, e.g., particle size images, particle size vs. measurement angle, etc., will be explored with multivariate data analysis tools to test the possibility to obtain additional information on the variation of particle size along the development of the process. Data fusion approaches can be envisioned in the framework of multisensor platforms. To do so, we will consider strategies to weight the relevance of the different sensor responses in the global response. Data fusion at different levels and multiset or multiblock analysis methods to deal with data from different origins will be tested to find the best approach for the different applications. URV and UB will jointly coordinate these tasks. Through URV-UB, we will develop software for the suitable sensor platforms. URV will focus mainly on developing chemometric models for the MEMS-NIR sensor for all the products of interest for laboratory data, and models will be developed for pilot-plant scale, with an optimised version delivered for its integration with the rest of the hardware in WP4. UB will mainly focus on process modelling tools and multivariate analysis tools to interpret the scatterometer output. Tailored data fusion strategies for multisensor platforms will be designed.

Page 2 of 84 SPIRE-06-2015 1. Excellence 1.1. Objectives "One company's waste and by-products, can be another company's opportunity and cost saving. We need to put those two companies in touch so that we don't just throw away valuable materials but make the most of them." Janez Potočnik, European Commissioner for the Environment Resource efficiency offers major economic opportunities for the European Process Industry, both in terms of cost savings as well as opportunities to offer greener products and services. According to a recent estimate, the EU could realistically reduce the total material requirements of its economy by 17% to 24%, boosting GDP and creating between 1.4 and 2.8 million jobs1. In order to migrate to the energy and resource efficient zero -waste process industry of the future, the SPIRE work programme calls for “ solutions for more efficient processing and energy systems for the process industry, including industrial symbiosis”. Industrial symbiosis (IS) is the use by one company or sector of b y-products, including energy, water, logistics and materials, from another. IS networks have proven successful not only in diverting waste from landfill, but also in contributing to the preservation of resources and moving waste up the value chain. They have also been an accelerator of innovation and creation of green jobs. The European Resource Efficiency Platform has championed IS as a mechanism for reducing carbon, preserving critical resources and securing business sustainability. However, inadequate business-to-business information on, for example, what resources a product or process contains hinders efficient material flows and the creation of value in the circular economy. Objectives In order to pave the way forward for industrial symbiosis as a “solution for more efficient processing and energy systems for the process industry”2, this project will develop a secure platform for the flexible management of shared process resources (SHAREBOX- figure 1) that will provide plant operations and production m anagers with the robust and reliable information that they need in real -time in order to effectively and confidently share resources (plant, energy, water, residues, and recycled materials) with other companies in an optimum symbiotic eco-system. The Platform will consist of a module for facilitating the identification and realisation of new cross -sectorial interactions (‘synergies’), that draws upon the expertise of partner ISL who have designed a standalone database software from partner ISL (SYNERGie®) in commercial use around the world supporting facilitated industrial symbiosis. This module will be embedded in the SHAREBOX Controller, which will consist of a suite of newly developed analysis and optimisation tools for flexible energy use and material flow integration for optimising symbiosis among companies. These tools will be based on input-output (IO) modelling for resource (waste and energy) supply-demand matching and process efficiency analysis (to understand physical and technological conditions), game theoretical (GT) approach for integrating company behaviour in cost -, benefit -, and resource -sharing (to understand economic conditions), and agent -based modelling (ABM) for designing the (economic, environmental, and social) optimal symbiotic network (to have the holistic optimum). The outputs from the SHAREBOX controller will provide plant and operations managers with commands for actions to be taken and/or recommendations for decision support. It will be ensured that all commands and recommendations a) fulfil plant operations requirements, b) are within the constraints of any contractual obligations, c) are in compliance with all regulatory thresholds, and d) deliver optimal impacts in terms of cost/savings and ecological footprints. The historical data that is generated by the SHAREBOX platform will be processed by data mining tools that will provide the production/process BIG DATA for symbiotic shared resources optimisation. The European process industry is uniquely positioned and represents the economic roots of the European economy (by transforming raw materials into intermediate and end-user products but also using energy, water, and possibly producing by-products, emissions and wastes)2. It thus sits at the core of most industrial value chains via discrete manufacturing into a whole host of sectors2. The process industry is characterised by having a high dependence on resources (energy, raw materials and water) in production. Optimising the effectiveness of process control systems and strategies is vital to improve quality, reliability and efficiency3, as well as the sustainability of operations. 3 http://www.spire2030.eu/mediaroom/36/21/Process-Control-Industrial-Automation-Summit-2014 Page 7 of 84 SPIRE-06-2015 1.2. Relation to the work programme SHAREBOX mobilises a multidisciplinary team who will work together to respond to the specific challenge of topic SPIRE-06-2015: Energy and resource management systems for improved efficiency in the process industries, whereby we propose to develop a highly scalable, secure platform for: a) Identification of new synergies . The upgraded SYNERGie® module that will be integrated into the SHAREBOX platform will enable next generation IS delivery through the ‘smart’ identification and real isation of new cross -sectorial interactions that will provide the foundations to enable ‘a more general cross -sectorial interaction’ by providing the next generation of IS delivery. b) Optimising IS implementation. The SHAREBOX intelligent decision support t ools for the flexible management of shared process resources will provide plant operations and production managers with the robust and reliable real-time needed to optimise symbiotic connections (plant, energy, water, residues, and recycled materials) with other companies in a symbiotic eco-system. The SHAREBOX project also develops ‘new approaches that perform cost-saving optimisation of energy and resources supply and demand’. Based on the input-output approach, SHAREBOX will implement the optimum IS as a pre-design model for future symbiotic networks (SNs) and use it as the theoretical optimum that the existing SNs should reach. Based on a game theory approach SHAREBOX will construct the benefit-sharing schemes for involved actors integrating the potenti al cooperation strategies in the negotiation phase of IS . Through an agent -based modelling approach SHAREBOX will enable the optimum IS design embedding the company behaviour into the operations of IS. This ‘holistic’ integrated platform will be a highly scalable and will ‘ integrate into a single management system” all the necessary ‘environmental, energy and economic factors’, as well as regulatory and contractual requirements, to ensure optimised industrial symbiosis. Consequently, it will be a key platform for improving the resource and energy efficiency of the process industries. As identified by SPIRE-06, a key challenge that is preventing the development of technical and non-technical interactions and exchanges is that of ‘poor understanding of each other's processes’. Behind this challenge is a lack of trust among industry to cooperate at a level which could provide visibility on how their operations are run . Indeed, lack of cooperation and trust, as well as lack of information, are frequently cited among the key barriers to industrial symbiosis4,5. This is a common and longstanding phenomenon in competitive industrial environments. However, nowadays, with the advancement in information and communication technology, information sharing can be more co nceivable5. To this end, one way to overcome the willingness to share information, and in particular potentially sensitive process information, is to develop an information sharing platform that offers outstanding security and anonymity prior to the agreement to progress an idea . We propose to do this through APIs for secure data exchange between companies. Moreover, the platform will properly encrypt or anonymize the data that is being fed into the system so that it does not reveal itself to other end-users, rather it is used intelligently by the platform’s models and tools that will process the data for the purpose of ‘ flexible energy use and material flow integration for holistic resource management in process industries’. The highly flexible and easy to use platform will be ‘suitable both for small and large scale in a flexible approach’. It may be implemented for the supply being generated by a park operator at first step and then being extended to the entire resource flows of a production site; or it may be used by a single tenant, then inviting neighbouring companies to join gradually. This way, SHAREBOX can be used as a nucleus for the continuous development of site integration advantages.
